Lancang-Mekong
Green Pulse

New Life proved it. Green Pulse scales it.

We are a four-member youth team turning campus textile waste into new materials, real climate data, and care for people in need. Not a second hand shop, but a green current protecting the Mekong that feeds 70 million lives across six countries.

Who we are

Four students, one green mission

We are a team from the Faculty of International Studies at Hanoi University, running Green Pulse together.

Where we came from

From a Campus Corner to a Six-Country Movement

Our journey began with a single bin of unwanted clothes and grew into a vision reaching across the Mekong region.

1
Where it started

A pile of still good clothes

On our own campus, usable clothing piled up in bins every semester. Bought cheap, worn twice, thrown out. Behind each piece sat invisible litres of Mekong water and a trail of dye, but no one was linking a student's closet to the health of the river. We decided to be the ones who did.

2
Sep 2024 to Feb 2026, our 18 month pilot

We built New Life for Old Items

Instead of just talking, we opened a real pop up store on campus. We set up collection points, sorted every item by hand, sold pieces at gentle prices, and gave clothing to families and children in need. It was small and unglamorous, but for eighteen months it actually ran.

3
What the pilot proved

Numbers we can stand behind

320 kgTextiles saved
92%Reuse rate
960,000 LVirtual water saved
78%Donor return rate

That last figure, 78% of donors returning, mattered most. It told us people trusted this and wanted it to grow.

4
Today

Lancang-Mekong Green Pulse

So we rebuilt the same warm idea as real infrastructure. We added AI sorting, a tamper evident ledger that turns every item into climate data, and a circular supply chain designed to reach all six Mekong countries: Vietnam, China (Yunnan), Laos, Thailand, Cambodia and Myanmar. New Life proved it. Now Green Pulse scales it.

Why this matters

Textile waste is a water problem in disguise

The Mekong sustains about 70 million people. Fashion is quietly one of its heaviest burdens, and the damage runs in a chain.

💦

Drained before it is worn

About 60% of a garment's water footprint is spent before harvest. Across the basin, that is billions of litres locked into clothes discarded within a year.

🐟

Dye, then dying water

Azo dyes and untreated runoff raise the river's load. Oxygen drops, fish struggle, and microplastics from synthetic fibres travel into the food chain.

🤝

People at the river's edge

Fishers, farmers and the families who depend on them sit at the end of this chain. Protecting the water is, in the end, about protecting them.

What we actually do

One garment, three streams of good

A thrift shop has one output: clothes to wear again. Green Pulse sends every item into three streams at once, and that is exactly what makes it a supply chain, not a store.

Material stream

Waste becomes feedstock

Wearable items go to people who need them. The rest is sorted by fibre and sent to industry as recycled cotton and polyester, raw material for a market that is growing fast across the region.

Data stream

A garment becomes a record

Every item is logged on a tamper evident ledger: weight, fibre, water and carbon saved. The system makes real, checkable climate data, something a shop can never offer.

Value stream

A small act becomes a credit

One donation becomes Green Pulse credits for the giver and reusable evidence for governments reporting on their climate goals, turning a private act into a public good.

The engine

Mekong Vision AI

Our sorting brain reads each textile's molecular signature with near infrared light and a neural network, finding the fibre type and blend so the right material reaches the right recycling stream. Try the live tool on its own site.

Open Mekong Vision AI ↗
Who we help

Three kinds of good, on purpose

Green Pulse is built so one action ripples outward, to the river, to people who need work, and to those who need a hand the most.

🌏 The environment

Healing the Lancang-Mekong

Every kilogram diverted is water saved, dye kept out of the river, and microplastics that never reach the food chain. We measure it, log it, and report it, so the impact is real, not just words.

🌾 Livelihoods

Work in a circular economy

The chain creates real roles: collection, sorting, design, data care. Student designers turn recovered fabric into products under our own label, and informal collectors find a fair, dignified place in the system.

💞 Vulnerable groups

Warmth for those who need it

Through our charity drives, wearable clothing and joyful visits reach children and families in need. One of them is Xuân Ấm, meaning Warm Spring, a highland community we have supported from the very start. Caring for the planet and caring for people are the same act here.

What we have done

Proof you can see

Most ideas stay on paper. Ours has already run. Here is a glimpse of the people and the work behind the numbers.

In good company

Friends and supporters

Where it all began

New Life for Old Items

Green Pulse grew out of New Life for Old Items, our 18 month campus pilot. It is not simply a partner, it is our root and our proof. Everything we do today started at this little green storefront.

Now recruiting new members

Become a Resource Steward

We welcome a new cohort of members every semester. You do not just volunteer, you help run real circular economy work, and you build skills most students never get to touch.

  • 🌱Hands on work across sorting, design, data and field operations. Pick where you want to grow.
  • 🤖Get close to real AI, ledger and climate policy work, not just theory.
  • 🌏Join a network reaching across the six Mekong countries.
  • 📜A certificate of contribution and a real line on your CV.
Thank you. Your application is in, and we will be in touch soon.
How it works

Bringing your textiles to Green Pulse

Whether you are a student donor or a partner, here is the journey your clothing takes, and what you get back.

1

You drop it off

Bring clean, unwanted textiles to a campus point or a Green Hub. Every item is weighed and registered.

2

We sort it

Mekong Vision AI reads the fibre and condition, so each piece goes to the best possible next life.

3

It finds a purpose

Wearable items reach people in need. The rest becomes industrial feedstock instead of landfill.

4

You see the impact

Earn Green Pulse credits and see the water and carbon your donation saved on our ledger.

Do I need to wash clothes before donating?

Yes please, bring items clean and dry. It keeps our sorting safe and lets more pieces go straight to reuse.

What can I donate?

Most clothing and household textiles. Even worn items are welcome. If they cannot be reused, they become feedstock rather than waste.

What are Green Pulse credits?

They are friendly reward points that say thank you for your contribution. They are not a cryptocurrency and cannot be traded. Think of them as a record of your impact.

Can my group partner with Green Pulse?

Of course. Brands, faculties and community groups can run collection drives or use our system to meet their sustainability goals. Say hello through the contact form below.

Say hello

Let us keep the river moving

Questions, partnerships, or just want to help? We would love to hear from you.

The problem

A shirt is a piece of the river

One cotton t-shirt holds about 2,700 litres of water, much of it drawn from the Mekong basin. When it is thrown away after being worn only a few times, that water is lost for nothing.

Multiply that by a million students across six countries and a small daily habit becomes a slow drain on a river that feeds 70 million people. Textile waste is not just rubbish. It is a hidden water and pollution problem.

What we do

One garment, three streams of good

A thrift shop has one output: clothes to wear again. Green Pulse sends every item into three streams at the same time.

  • Material: wearable pieces go to people in need, the rest becomes recycled cotton and polyester for industry.
  • Data: every item is logged on a ledger with its weight, fibre, water and carbon saved.
  • Value: each donation earns Green Pulse credits and creates evidence governments can use for climate reporting.

That is what makes us a green supply chain, not a store.

The engine

Mekong Vision AI

Sorting textiles by hand is slow and often wrong. Mekong Vision AI reads each item's molecular signature with near infrared light, then a neural network identifies the fibre type and blend, so the right material reaches the right recycling stream.

This is the technology behind our data stream, and it has its own live tool you can try.

Open Mekong Vision AI ↗

Green Pulse credits

A friendly reward for every good act

When you donate, you earn Green Pulse credits. The amount reflects the real water and carbon your item helped save, verified on our ledger, so every credit stands for a true piece of impact.

You never see the maths. You just see something like "you saved 1,500 litres of water" and watch your rank on our river climb from Raindrop to Stream to River. Credits are a thank you and a record, not money, and they cannot be traded.

Spend them on discounts, workshops, events and member perks.

Our impact

Three kinds of good, on purpose

  • The river: every kilogram diverted means water saved and dye kept out of the Mekong.
  • Livelihoods: the chain creates real roles in collection, sorting, design and data.
  • People in need: wearable clothing and joyful visits reach children and families, including the Xuân Ấm highland community.

Caring for the planet and caring for people are the same act here.

Where we started

The New Life story

Green Pulse grew out of New Life for Old Items, an 18 month pop up store we ran on our own campus. We set up collection points, sorted clothes by hand, sold pieces gently and gave to people in need.

It kept 320 kg of textiles in use, reached a 92% reuse rate, saved around 960,000 litres of water, and brought 78% of donors back. New Life proved the idea could work. Green Pulse is how we make it grow.
